sentence_reviews.text: Coordinates, phases, or learned positions inject order, time, or location into models whose base computation is otherwise permutation-equivariant or location-agnostic.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Vaswani et al. 2017, Section 3.5 on positional encodings; NeRF paper/reference for coordinate-based neural fields; PML general representation-learning context.
sentence_reviews.notes: Transformer positional encodings and coordinate-based fields support the idea that order/location is injected through extra encodings. The broader phrasing over time, phase, and location-agnostic base computations is a synthesis rather than a single-source statement.

sentence_reviews.text: The role of Positional, temporal & coordinate encodings is to connect Sequences, events & time series, Embeddings & distributed representations to downstream capabilities including Relative position, RoPE, ALiBi & coordinate mechanisms, Neural fields & radiance-field models.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Transformer Section 3.5 for sequence position encodings; NeRF reference for coordinate inputs to radiance fields; PML for embeddings/representation background.
sentence_reviews.notes: The sources support positional encodings for sequence models and coordinate encodings for neural fields, but the exact graph-derived downstream list, including RoPE and ALiBi, is not directly supported by the cited candidate references.

sentence_reviews.text: substrate for Relative position, RoPE, ALiBi & coordinate mechanisms
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Vaswani et al. 2017, Section 3.5 describes positional encodings for sequence order.
sentence_reviews.notes: The source supports positional encodings generally, but not the specific RoPE and ALiBi mechanisms named in the sentence.

sentence_reviews.text: substrate for Neural fields & radiance-field models
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: NeRF reference describes representing scenes as functions of spatial position and viewing direction.
sentence_reviews.notes: Coordinate inputs are central to neural radiance fields. 'Substrate for' is atlas wording, so this is synthesis rather than direct wording.

sentence_reviews.text: Encoding choices can discard information, amplify bias, or make important invariances difficult to learn. Its direct dependencies include Sequences, events & time series, Embeddings & distributed representations.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: PML representation-learning discussions are relevant but do not directly make this full limitation claim for positional encodings.
sentence_reviews.notes: This appears to be generic generated limitation text. The cited packet sources do not support the exact claims about discarded information, amplified bias, invariances, or the dependency list.

sentence_reviews.text: Positional, temporal & coordinate encodings consumes, emits, or organizes information using Sequences, events & time series.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Transformer Section 3.5 describes positional encodings because sequence models need token order information.
sentence_reviews.notes: Sequence order is directly relevant, but the rationale wording is graph-derived.

sentence_reviews.text: Positional, temporal & coordinate encodings consumes, emits, or organizes information using Embeddings & distributed representations.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Transformer Section 3.5 adds positional encodings to input embeddings; PML covers learned representations generally.
sentence_reviews.notes: Adding positional encodings to embeddings is directly supported for transformers; the more general relation is a synthesis.

sentence_reviews.text: Positional, temporal & coordinate encodings rests on the formal or computational capabilities supplied by Geometry, topology & symmetry.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: NeRF reference uses spatial coordinates and viewing directions for scene representation.
sentence_reviews.notes: Spatial coordinate use supports geometry relevance, but topology and symmetry are not directly supported by the cited evidence.

edge_reviews.notes: Sequence order is a core motivation for positional encodings in transformers. Direction and type are defensible.
Transformer positional encodings are combined with token embeddings, supporting a core representation relation.
Coordinate encodings support a geometry dependency for some uses, but the broad geometry/topology/symmetry dependency is not established for all positional encodings.
